name: Heise
description: A fully intuitive method created by Ryan Heise in 2003 that requires zero algorithms. The Heise method builds four 1x2x2 blocks, orients edges, then uses commutators to finish the solve. It achieves an impressively low move count through pure understanding of cube mechanics rather than memorized sequences. Regarded as one of the most elegant methods in cubing, it is primarily used for fewest-moves challenges and by those seeking a deep understanding of the puzzle.

steps.name: Build Four 1x2x2 Blocks
steps.description: Create four separate 1x2x2 blocks (each consisting of one corner and one edge) and arrange them so that two blocks share an edge on each side. This forms the skeleton of the solve while leaving flexibility for efficient solutions.
tips: Look for blocks that already exist in the scramble
Plan as many blocks as possible during inspection
Blocks do not need to be placed in final position yet

steps.name: Orient Remaining Edges
steps.description: Orient all remaining edges that are not part of the four blocks. Edge orientation is defined relative to the F/B axis, and this step ensures all edges can be solved without F or B moves.
tips: Edge orientation should be considered during block building
Sometimes EO can be solved for free during block construction
Fewer bad edges means fewer moves

steps.name: Solve Edges and Two Corners
steps.description: Place all remaining edges and two of the remaining corners into their correct positions. This is done intuitively by matching edges to blocks and inserting corner-edge pairs. After this step, only three corners remain unsolved.
tips: Think of this as completing F2L minus three corners
Use blockbuilding techniques to pair and insert pieces
Leave corners that form an easy 3-cycle for the last step

steps.name: 3-Corner Commutator
steps.description: Solve the final three corners using a commutator. A commutator is a sequence of moves in the form A B A' B' that cycles three pieces without disturbing the rest of the cube. This step requires understanding of how corner pieces move.
tips: Learn basic commutator structures (A B A' B')
Setup moves can simplify the commutator
Practice finding short commutators for different corner positions

pros: Develops deep understanding of cube mechanics
cons: Extremely difficult to learn and master
Very slow turning speed due to constant thinking
creator: Ryan Heise
